The **regular script**[[a]](#cite_note-names-1) is the newest of the major [Chinese script styles](/wiki/Chinese_script_styles "Chinese script styles"), emerging during the [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms "Three Kingdoms") period c. 230 CE, and stylistically mature by the 7th century. It is the most common style used in modern text. In its traditional form it is the third-most common in publishing after the [Ming](/wiki/Ming_typefaces "Ming typefaces") and [Gothic](/wiki/East_Asian_Gothic_typeface "East Asian Gothic typeface") types used exclusively in print.[[1]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regular_script&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The *Xuanhe Calligraphy Manual* (宣和書譜) credits [Wang Cizhong](/w/index.php?title=Wang_Cizhong&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wang Cizhong (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%8E%8B%E6%AC%A1%E4%BB%B2 "zh:王次仲")] with creating the regular script, based on the [clerical script](/wiki/Clerical_script "Clerical script") of the early [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") (202 BCE – 220 CE). It became popular during the [Eastern Han](/wiki/Eastern_Han "Eastern Han") and Three Kingdoms periods,[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000143-3) with [Zhong Yao](/wiki/Zhong_Yao "Zhong Yao") (c. 151 – 230),[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000142-4) a calligrapher in the state of [Cao Wei](/wiki/Cao_Wei "Cao Wei") (220–266), being credited as its first master, known as the father of regular script. His famous works include the *Xuanshi biao* (宣示表), *Jianjizhi biao* (薦季直表), and *Liming biao* (力命表). Palaeographer [Qiu Xigui](/wiki/Qiu_Xigui "Qiu Xigui")[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000143-3) describes the script in *Xuanshi biao* as:

> ...clearly emerging from the womb of early period semi-cursive script. If one were to write the tidily written variety of early period semi-cursive script in a more dignified fashion and were to use consistently the pause technique [(頓; *dùn*)], used to reinforce the beginning or ending of a stroke when ending horizontal strokes, a practice which already appears in early period semi-cursive script, and further were to make use of right-falling strokes with thick feet, the result would be a style of calligraphy like that in the "Xuān shì biǎo".

However, very few wrote in this script at the time other than a few literati; most continued writing in the neo-clerical script, or a hybrid form of semi-cursive and neo-clerical.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000143-3) The regular script did not become dominant until the 5th century during the early [Northern and Southern period](/wiki/Northern_and_Southern_period "Northern and Southern period") (420–589); there was a variety of the regular script which emerged from neo-clerical as well as regular scripts[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000146-5) known as '[Wei](/wiki/Northern_Wei "Northern Wei") regular' (魏楷; *Wèikǎi*) or 'Wei stele' (魏碑; *Wèibēi*). Thus, the regular script is descended both from the early semi-cursive style as well as from the neo-clerical script.

The script is considered to have become stylistically mature during the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") (618–907), with the most famous and oft-imitated calligraphers of that period being the early Tang's Four Great Calligraphers (初唐四大家): [Ouyang Xun](/wiki/Ouyang_Xun "Ouyang Xun"), [Yu Shinan](/wiki/Yu_Shinan "Yu Shinan"), [Chu Suiliang](/wiki/Chu_Suiliang "Chu Suiliang"), and [Xue Ji](/wiki/Xue_Ji "Xue Ji"), as well as the tandem of [Yan Zhenqing](/wiki/Yan_Zhenqing "Yan Zhenqing") and [Liu Gongquan](/wiki/Liu_Gongquan "Liu Gongquan").

During the [Northern Song](/wiki/Northern_Song "Northern Song") (960–1127), [Emperor Huizong](/wiki/Emperor_Huizong_of_Song "Emperor Huizong of Song") created an iconic style known as 'slender gold' (瘦金體; *shòujīntǐ*).[[5]](#cite_note-6) During the [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") (1271–1368), [Zhao Mengfu](/wiki/Zhao_Mengfu "Zhao Mengfu") (1254–1322) also became known for his own calligraphic style for the regular script, called *Zhaoti* (趙體).

92 rules governing the fundamental structure of regular script were established during the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") (1644–1912); the calligrapher [Huang Ziyuan](/w/index.php?title=Huang_Ziyuan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Huang Ziyuan (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E9%BB%84%E8%87%AA%E5%85%83 "zh:黄自元")] wrote a guidebook illustrating these rules, with four characters provided as an example for each.

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regular_script&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

The [Eight Principles of *Yong*](/wiki/Eight_Principles_of_Yong "Eight Principles of Yong") encapsulate varieties of most strokes that appear in the regular script.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi200965-7) Regular script characters with dimensions larger than 5 cm (2 in) are usually classified as 'large' (大楷; *dàkǎi*); those smaller than 2 cm (0.8 in) are usually classified as 'small' (小楷; *xiǎokǎi*), and those in between are 'medium' (中楷; *zhōngkǎi*).[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi200931-8)

Notable works written in regular script include the Northern and Southern-era *Records of Yao Boduo Sculpturing* (姚伯多造像記) and *Tablet of General Guangwu* (廣武將軍碑), the Sui-era *Tablet of Longzang Temple* (龍藏寺碑), *Tombstone Record of Sui Xiaoci* (蘇孝慈墓誌), and *Tombstone Record of Beauty Tong* (董美人墓誌), and the Tang-era *Sweet Spring at Jiucheng Palace* (九成宮醴泉銘).

## Derivative styles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regular_script&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Derivative styles")]

* [Fangsong](/wiki/Fangsong "Fangsong") typefaces are based on a printed style which developed during the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") (970–1279)
* The most common printed typeface styles, [Ming](/wiki/Ming_typefaces "Ming typefaces") and [sans-serif](/wiki/East_Asian_Gothic_typeface "East Asian Gothic typeface"), are based on Fangsong
* Japanese textbook typefaces *kyōkashotai* (教科書体) are based on regular script, but modified so that they appear to be written with a pencil or pen. They also follow the *[jōyō kanji](/wiki/J%C5%8Dy%C5%8D_kanji "Jōyō kanji")* character forms.
* The [bopomofo](/wiki/Bopomofo "Bopomofo") semi-syllabograms are nearly always written using regular script strokes.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regular_script&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-names_1-0)** Regular script is referred to by several related names in Chinese, including 楷書; 楷书; *kǎishū*, 正楷; *zhèngkǎi*, 真書; 真书; *zhēnshū*, 楷體; 楷体; *kǎitǐ*, and 正書; 正书; *zhèngshū*. It is sometimes called *block script*,[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGao200941-10) *standard script*, or *square style* in English.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-shengjiaoxu_9-0)** 

   其數然而天地苞  
   乎陰陽而易識者  
   以其有象也陰陽  
   處乎天地而難窮
